en.extract:
The white-browed wagtail or large pied wagtail (Motacilla maderaspatensis) is a medium-sized bird and is the largest member of the wagtail family. They are conspicuously patterned with black above and white below, a prominent white brow, shoulder stripe and outer tail feathers. White-browed wagtails are native to the Indian subcontinent, common near small water bodies and have adapted to urban environments where they often nest on roof tops. The specific name is derived from the Indian city of Madras (now Chennai).


Taxonomy
The white-browed wagtail was formally described in 1789 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the wagtails in the genus Motacilla and coined the binomial name Motacilla maderaspatensis. The specific epithet is Modern Latin for the locality, Madras (or Madraspattanam and now Chennai) in southern India. Gmelin's account was based on the "black and white wagtail" that had been described and illustrated in 1713 by the English naturalist John Ray. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The white-browed wagtail is the largest species of wagtail at 21 cm (8.3 in) length. It is a slender bird, with the characteristic long, constantly wagging tail of its genus. It has black upperparts, head and breast, with a white supercilium and large white wingbar. Unlike white wagtails it never has white on the forehead. The rest of the underparts are white.  The female has the black less intense than in the male. Juveniles are like the females brown-grey where the adult is black.


Distribution and habitat

The white-browed wagtail is a resident breeder in India and is endemic to the Indian subcontinent. It is found south of the Himalayas, east of the Indus system and to the west of Bangladesh. It is rare in the higher altitude regions but has been seen in Ladakh on the edge of the Tibetan plateau. In most of India it is found below 1000 m but in southern India it goes up into the hills up to 2200 m. It is very rare in the Indus valley area. It is absent from the Sind region of Pakistan. It is found in open freshwater wetland habitats. It is one of the few Motacilla wagtails that has adapted well to urban habitats and is often found perched on overhead water storages in residential buildings.
It is a rare winter visitor to Sri Lanka and have possibly extended their range in recent times.


Taxonomy and systematics
Stuart Baker in his second edition of The Fauna of British India considered this as a subspecies of the white wagtail, calling it Motacilla lugubris maderaspatensis. This was however criticized by C B Ticehurst, who noted that it was much larger, never had the white forehead of the white wagtail, was non-migratory and lacked a spring moult. This species is now considered to form a superspecies with Japanese wagtail, Mekong wagtail and the more distant African pied wagtail. Similarities in pre-copulatory behaviour with the Japanese wagtail have been noted. Its song much resembles that of the recently described Mekong wagtail. mtDNA cytochrome b and NADH dehydrogenase subunit 2 sequence data has not been able to robustly resolve the relationships of these birds, especially in respect to the blue-headed wagtail and its relatives.
The species is considered monotypic and subspecies such as kangrae described by Walter Koelz are not recognized.


Behaviour and ecology
Usually seen in pairs or small groups near open water. They call often especially in the mornings and are active like most other wagtails. They will perch on the ground as well as on wires or on buildings. The song is long and loud with many different notes. The usual call is a wheezy "wheech". They can fly fairly rapidly for long distances and they fly with a bounding (dipping and rising) flight pattern and have been recorded to travel at the speed of about 40 km/h.
Endoparasitic filarial parasites of the species Splendidofilaria singhi have been recorded in individuals of the species.


Breeding

The breeding season is March to October. In southern India, the nesting season begins when river levels drop and continue until the Monsoon rains. In courtship, the male shoots into the air with a single wing beat, sings and glides with dangling legs and puffed feathers. On settling, the tail of the male is raised high and wings held up over the back and the tip is quivered stiffly while he steps around the female. The female responds by crouching and shivering the wings as if begging for food.
It builds its cup-shaped nest placed on the ground or rocks in a hole, ledge or mud bank and is always located close to water. Artificial structures such as bridges and roof tops are also used. Nests have been noticed in a regularly used ferry. The nest is made of grass, roots, algae and other material with a central neat cup lined with hair. Normally four, and three to five eggs is the usual clutch.


Food
Like other wagtails, this species is insectivorous. Nestlings were mainly fed orthopterans, caterpillars and spiders. Stayphylinid beetles and pentatomid bugs have also been recorded in their diet. They have adapted to urban environments where water may be found mainly on rooftops as overflow from storage tanks.


In culture
In older times in India, the species was sometimes kept as a cage-bird and was acclaimed for its singing ability. The native name of khanjan is used in the phrase "khanjan-eyed" to describe someone with beautiful eyes. The Khanjan was held sacred and considered a good omen in India as it supposedly bore an impression of Vishnu's shaligram on its breast. A variety of beliefs on the future predicted by where the bird sat and what it did have been documented by Saratchandra Mitra. Another local name for wagtails in India is dhobin (or washerwoman).


References


Further reading

It is a slender bird, with the characteristic long, constantly wagging tail of its genus. It has black upperparts, head and breast, with a white supercilium and large white wingbar. Unlike [[white wagtail]]s it never has white on the forehead. The rest of the underparts are white. The female has the black less intense than in the male. Juveniles are like the females brown-grey where the adult is black.{{Cite book|pages=291–293|url=https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin02oate#page/291/mode/1up/|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds| volume=2| year=1890| author=Oates, EW|publisher= Taylor and Francis, London}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/popularhandbooko033226mbp#page/n292/mode/1up/ |author=Whistler, Hugh|year=1949|title=Popular handbook of Indian birds |pages=245–246|edition=4| publisher=Gurney and Jackson, London|isbn=1-4067-4576-6}} ==Distribution and habitat== [[File:White-browed Wagtail (Motacilla maderaspatensis) carrying feed for the chicks W IMG 2622.jpg|thumb|left|Foraging on a water lily leaf]] The white-browed wagtail is a resident breeder in [[India]] and is [[endemism|endemic]] to the Indian subcontinent. It is found south of the Himalayas, east of the Indus system and to the west of Bangladesh. It is rare in the higher altitude regions but has been seen in [[Ladakh]] on the edge of the Tibetan plateau.{{Cite journal|author=Sangha, H. S. Naoroji, R. |year= 2005 |title= Large Pied Wagtail ''Motacilla maderaspatensis'' Gmelin in Ladakh|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=102|issue=3|page=341|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48376006}} In most of India it is found below 1000 m but in southern India it goes up into the hills up to 2200 m. It is very rare in the Indus valley area.{{Cite journal |author=Whistler, H| year=1916| title= Notes on the birds of the Jhelum District of the Punjab with notes on the collection by Claud Ticehurst|journal= Ibis | volume=58|pages=35–118| url=https://archive.org/stream/ibis1041916brit#page/72/mode/2up/search/maderaspatensis|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1916.tb07842.x}} It is absent from the Sind region of Pakistan. It is found in open freshwater wetland habitats. It is one of the few ''Motacilla'' wagtails that has adapted well to urban habitats and is often found perched on overhead water storages in residential buildings.{{Cite journal|journal=Forktail| volume =21|year=2005| pages=79–86| title=Species diversity, relative abundance and habitat use of the birds in the Sundarbans East Wildlife Sanctuary, Bangladesh|author=Khan, MMH|url=http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/21pdf/Khan-Sundarbans.pdf}} It is a rare winter visitor to Sri Lanka and have possibly extended their range in recent times.{{Cite journal|author=Hoffmann, Thilo W |year=1989| title= Notes on the status and distribution of some birds in Sri Lanka as listed in S. D. Ripley (1982) "A Synopsis of the Birds of India and Pakistan together with those of Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka."|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=86|issue=1|pages=7–16|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710404}} ==Taxonomy and systematics== [[Edward Charles Stuart Baker|Stuart Baker]] in his second edition of ''The Fauna of British India'' considered this as a subspecies of the [[white wagtail]], calling it ''Motacilla lugubris maderaspatensis''.{{Cite book|year=2005| title= Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|page=315|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions |author1=Rasmussen, PC |author2=JC Anderton |name-list-style=amp }}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds3/BakerFBI3#page/n288/mode/1up|pages= 263–264|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds|volume=3|edition=2|author=Baker, ECS|year=1926|publisher=Taylor & Francis, London}} This was however criticized by [[Claud Buchanan Ticehurst|C B Ticehurst]], who noted that it was much larger, never had the white forehead of the white wagtail, was non-migratory and lacked a spring moult.{{Cite journal|author=Ticehurst, CB |year=1922|title= Notes on Indian wagtails|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=28|issue=4|pages=1082–1090|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/52170736}} This [[species]] is now considered to form a [[superspecies]] with [[Japanese wagtail]], [[Mekong wagtail]] and the more distant [[African pied wagtail]]. Similarities in pre-copulatory behaviour with the Japanese wagtail have been noted.{{Cite journal|author1=Higuchi H |author2=T Hirano |name-list-style=amp |year=1989|title=Breeding season, courtship behaviour, and territoriality of White and Japanese Wagtails ''Motacilla alba'' and ''M. randis''|journal=Ibis|volume=131|issue=4|pages=578–588|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1989.tb04793.x|url=https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/2027.42/73846/1/j.1474-919X.1989.tb04793.x.pdf|hdl=2027.42/73846 |hdl-access=free}} Its song much resembles that of the recently described [[Mekong wagtail]]. [[mtDNA]] [[cytochrome b|cytochrome ''b'']] and [[NADH dehydrogenase]] [[Protein subunit|subunit]] 2 [[DNA sequence|sequence]] data has not been able to robustly resolve the relationships of these birds, especially in respect to the [[western yellow wagtail|blue-headed wagtail]] and its relatives.{{Cite journal|author=Voelker, Gary |year=2002|title= Systematics and historical biogeography of wagtails: Dispersal versus vicariance revisited|journal=Condor|volume=104|issue=4| pages=725–739| doi=10.1650/0010-5422(2002)104[0725:SAHBOW]2.0.CO;2|s2cid=85844839 |doi-access=free}} The species is considered monotypic and subspecies such as ''kangrae'' described by [[Walter Norman Koelz|Walter Koelz]] are not recognized.{{Cite journal|author=Koelz, Walter|year=1939| title= New birds from Asia, chiefly from India| journal= Proc. Biol. Soc. Washington|volume=52|pages=61–82}}{{Cite book|hdl=2027.42/56418|author=Storer, RW|title=Type specimens of birds in the collections of the University of Michigan Museum of Zoology. Miscellaneous Publications 174|year=1988|publisher=University of Michigan Museum of Zoology}} ==Behaviour and ecology== Usually seen in pairs or small groups near open water. They call often especially in the mornings{{Cite journal|author=Newnham, A |year=1898 |title=The Large Pied Wagtail ''Motacilla maderaspatensis'' in captivity|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=11|issue=3|pages=535–536|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30157293}} and are active like most other wagtails. They will perch on the ground as well as on wires or on buildings. The song is long and loud with many different notes. The usual call is a wheezy "wheech". They can fly fairly rapidly for long distances and they fly with a bounding (dipping and rising) flight pattern and have been recorded to travel at the speed of about 40 km/h.{{Cite journal|author= Acharya, H.N. |year=1933|title=Speed of the Large Pied Wagtail ''Motacilla maderaspatensis'' (Gmelin)|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=36|issue=4|pages=996–997|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48291912}} Endoparasitic filarial parasites of the species ''Splendidofilaria singhi'' have been recorded in individuals of the species.{{Cite journal|doi= 10.2307/3275259|title= New Species of ''Chandlerella'' Yorke and Maplestone, 1926, and ''Splendidofilaria'' Skrjabin, 1923, from Indian Birds, with a Discussion of Related Genera|jstor= 3275259| author=Sultana, A|journal= Journal of Parasitology |volume=48| issue= 5 |year= 1962|pages=707–714 }} ===Breeding=== [[File:White-browed Wagtail (Motacilla maderaspatensis) collecting waste material from the chicks W IMG 2647.jpg|thumb|White-browed wagtail at nest in [[Hyderabad]], India]] The breeding season is March to October. In southern India, the nesting season begins when river levels drop and continue until the Monsoon rains. In courtship, the male shoots into the air with a single wing beat, sings and glides with dangling legs and puffed feathers. On settling, the tail of the male is raised high and wings held up over the back and the tip is quivered stiffly while he steps around the female. The female responds by crouching and shivering the wings as if begging for food.{{Cite book|author1=Ali, S |author2=S D Ripley |name-list-style=amp | title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|edition=2|publisher=Oxford University Press|volume= 9|year= 1998 |pages =300–302|isbn=0-19-562063-1 }}{{Cite journal|author=Kirkpatrick, KM |year=1954 |title=The courtship display of the Large Pied Wagtail ''Motacilla maderaspatensis'' (Gmelin)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=52|issue=3|pages=602–603|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48184492}} It builds its cup-shaped nest placed on the ground or rocks in a hole, ledge or mud bank and is always located close to water. Artificial structures such as bridges and roof tops are also used.{{Cite journal|author=Biddulph, CH |year=1937 |title= Curious nesting sites of 'the Large Pied Wagtail [''Motacilla lugubris maderaspatensis'' (Gmel.)]|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume= 39|issue=2|pages=397–398|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47602145}} Nests have been noticed in a regularly used ferry. The nest is made of grass, roots, algae and other material with a central neat cup lined with hair. Normally four, and three to five eggs is the usual clutch.{{Cite book|pages=202–207| title= The nests and eggs of Indian birds|author=Hume, AO|edition=2|volume=2|year=1890|publisher= R H Porter, London|url=https://archive.org/stream/nestseggsofindia02hume#page/202/mode/1up/search}} ===Food=== Like other wagtails, this species is insectivorous. Nestlings were mainly fed [[orthoptera]]ns, [[caterpillar]]s and [[spider]]s.{{Cite journal|author=Verzhutskii, Boris |year=2002|title= Diet of the Large Pied Wagtail ''Motacilla maderaspatensis'' in Auroville, Pondicherry|journal= Zoos' Print Journal|volume=17|issue=9|page=886 |doi=10.11609/jott.zpj.17.9.886|doi-access=free}} [[Staphylinidae|Stayphylinid]] beetles and [[Pentatomidae|pentatomid]] bugs have also been recorded in their diet.{{Cite book|author=D'Abreu, E A |year=1920| chapter=Some insect prey of birds in the Central Provinces| title=Proceedings of the Third Entomological Meeting Held at Pusa on the 3rd to 15th February 1919|volume=3|pages=859–871|chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/reportofprocee03ento#page/859/mode/1up|publisher=Superintendent Government Printing, Calcutta}} They have adapted to urban environments where water may be found mainly on rooftops as overflow from storage tanks.{{Cite journal|author=Bell, D.A. |year=1986| title= An unusual nest site for the Large Pied Wagtail|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=26|issue=7&8|pages=11–12|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW26#page/n81/mode/1up}} ==In culture== In older times in India, the species was sometimes kept as a cage-bird and was acclaimed for its singing ability. The native name of ''khanjan'' is used in the phrase "khanjan-eyed" to describe someone with beautiful eyes.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/petbirdsofbengal00laws#page/240/mode/2up|pages=240–264|title=Pet birds of Bengal|volume=1|author=Law, Satya Churn|publisher=Thacker, Spink & Co|year=1923}} The Khanjan was held sacred and considered a good omen in India as it supposedly bore an impression of [[Vishnu]]'s [[shaligram]] on its breast.{{cite journal |journal= Chambers' Edinburgh Journal |issue=441 |year=1852 |url=https://www.gutenberg.org/files/24892/24892-h/24892-h.htm#THE_KHUNJUNEE |page=384 |title= The Khunjunee}} A variety of beliefs on the future predicted by where the bird sat and what it did have been documented by [[Saratchandra Mitra]].{{cite journal|author=Mitra, Carat Chandra|year=1898|title=Bengali and Behari Folk-lore about Birds. Part I.|journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal|volume=67|issue=2|pages=67–74}} Another local name for wagtails in India is ''dhobin'' (or washerwoman).{{Cite book|pages=106–107|title=Garden and aviary birds of India|author=Finn, Frank|url=https://archive.org/stream/gardenaviarybird00fin#page/107/mode/1up/|year=1906| publisher= Thacker and Spink}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== {{ref begin}} * Hussain, T., Ghafoor, A., Qureshi, J.I. (1989) Food habits of large pied wagtail (Motacilla maderaspatensis) . ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 38(5):88. {{ref end}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1583749}} {{DEFAULTSORT:White-Browed Wagtail}} [[Category:Motacilla]] [[Category:Birds of Nepal]] [[Category:Birds of Pakistan]] [[Category:Birds of India]] [[Category:Birds described in 1789]] [[Category:Taxa named by Johann Friedrich Gmelin]]

zh.extract:
印度鶺鸰（学名：Motacilla maderaspatensis）为鶺鸰科鶺鸰属的鸟类。分布于印度半岛、南部有印度库奇到坎尼亚库马里、往东至孟加拉湾以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在印度。
